learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate an understanding of basic economic principles and their application to agriculture
Understand and appreciate the relationship between biophysical and socio-economic information in the analysis of resource allocation within the agricultural sector
Use budgeting and economic analysis tools for farm and risk management and decision making
Understand the economics of agricultural markets and the use of economic concepts in the analysis of these markets
Apply economic analysis to the critical analysis of agricultural and resource policies

assessments.due_date:
1. Principles of economics & comparative advantage 7/08/2026 2:00 pm

2. Ag markets: supply, demand, welfare & elasticities 4/09/2026 2:00 pm

3. Production economics 23/10/2026 2:00 pm

Each problem set will be made available at least 48 hours before the due date.
assessments.task_description:
• problem sets:
  • are a take-home problem set task administered through the course's Learn.UQ site. Students may work on the assessment at their own pace, provided it is submitted by the stated due date time. Each problem set will be made available at least 48 hours before the due date.
  • test understanding of learning materials from the lectures and tutorials. 
  • use mostly scenario-based questions.
  • may include a mix of question types—some require short written answers, others involve calculations, and some may require a hand-drawn graph. Where applicable, a photo or scanned copy of the graph must be uploaded with instructions provided inside the problem set.
  • More details will be provided on the course's Learn.UQ site and in class.
  • Students are responsible for ensuring a stable internet connection while undertaking an online assessment
  • Unless specifically indicated during the semester by the course coordinator, marks and feedback from this assessment will be available within two weeks of the assessment’s due date.
  • Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. The use of AI or MT must be acknowledged appropriately. Please see assessment guidelines on the course's UQ.Learn site. Students may also refer to Library guides found under Additional assessment information > Acknowledging and referencing AI use.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This online assessment task must be submitted using the correct submission link in the course Learn.UQ site. An electronic copy of every piece of assessment submitted should be retained.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This report involves examining an agricultural policy issue (examples will be provided in class). Students will:

  • describe and analyse the current policy and its outcomes ("status quo" or "business as usual");
  • identify and economically evaluate alternative policy options; and
  • develop a justified policy recommendation based on the comparative analysis.

A topic may be selected from a provided list or proposed by the student. Full details on the assessment requirements and description will be available on the course's Learn.UQ site.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. The use of AI or MT must be acknowledged appropriately. Please see assessment guidelines on the course's UQ.Learn site. Students may also refer to Library guides found under Additional assessment information > Acknowledging and referencing AI use.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Further details about the exam will be provided in the lectures and on the course's Learn.UQ site.

The exam will be an on-campus exam. Have your UQ student ID card available for all your exams.

Students enrolled in In Person delivery must attend the exam in-person.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(27 Jul - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Series of applied classes

Please refer to Learn.UQ for weekly learning activities.


Sub-activity: Economic principles for agriculture: (1) Principles for and examples of the application of economics to agriculture (trade-offs, marginal analysis, and opportunity costs), (2) comparative advantage. Economics of agricultural markets: (1) supply and demand in agricultural markets, with an emphasis on how changes in supply and demand alter market prices and quantities, (2) how markets affect economic well-being (welfare economics), taxes and subsidies when there is no market failure, (3) responsiveness of one economic variable to a change in another (elasticities), externalities. Agricultural and resource policies: (1) positive production externalities, market failure, and the role of government intervention to alleviate market failure, (2) public goods, and government intervention in trade. Agricultural production economics: (1) input-output relationships and their application to agricultural and natural resource decision making, (2) optimal combination of inputs, (3) cost of production. Time allocated for preparing and revision for the final exam. Field trip and/or guest lecture.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
